text: Hello, I'm sharing a cholecystectomy in a gentleman who developed a calculus cholecystitis while recovering from open heart surgery a couple of months ago, was temporized with a percutaneous cholecystostomy tube. They have been optimized. They are off their anticoagulation for the procedure and they present for cholecystectomy.

The surgeon removes the cholecystostomy tube, allowing retraction of the gallbladder above the liver. Fatty tissue is cleared from the bottom of the gallbladder to expose the presumed location of the duct and artery.
The surgeon begins dissection by removing fat covering the base of the gallbladder and the presumed Calot's triangle, focusing on the lateral aspect of the gallbladder wall and progressing upwards.
The surgeon identifies the artery positioned anteriorly and uses an instrument to retract the fatty tissue medially, allowing for the opening of the peritoneum on the medial side.
The surgeon adopts a top-down approach to increase the mobility of the gallbladder, aiming to facilitate the dissection around the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon continues the dissection downwards, encountering a posterior artery. The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control the artery.

The surgeon returns to the bottom of the surgical field, attempting to identify the cystic duct emerging from a green structure believed to be the common duct. The surgeon continues thinning out the surrounding fat to expose the gallbladder wall.

The surgeon encircles the cystic artery, mobilizing it further to ensure it is not heading back into the liver. The artery is confirmed to be anteriorly based, with no other arterial structures visible emanating from the gallbladder into the liver.

The surgeon identifies the gallbladder wall and the cystic duct, working to safely thin out the surrounding tissue. The bifurcation of the cystic duct from the common duct is clearly visible as the surgeon approaches the gallbladder wall.
The surgeon identifies a clearer dissection plane at the bottom of the gallbladder, allowing for more confident and precise dissection in this area.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and continues to thin out the surrounding tissue, working posteriorly to safely dissect and separate it from the common duct. The area is now clearly visible, allowing for precise dissection.

The surgeon identifies the common duct and observes the gallbladder funneling down to the cystic duct. The surgeon continues to thin out the surrounding tissue to prepare for placing clips across the cystic duct.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery and cystic duct, which are the only two structures visible coming off the porta hepatis. The artery is then cut, followed by the duct.

The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy, ensuring meticulous hemostasis in the surgical area. The gallbladder has been successfully removed, and the surgical field is inspected for any bleeding before concluding the procedure.
